Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch Day 10 - The View

This time we're going to look at the View part of MVC, basically the visual layer. Last time we covered the basics of defining a Controller, so now it's the View's turn, and we'll get to the Model after that. We'll also pick up a little bit of HTML templating along the way.
folder (heads up: uppercase letters get converted to lowercase), and inside that create a
. Here's the starting content for the View:
language-html shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
Say Hi!

That's a basic View definition. But remember from the MVC architecture chapter: the user first requests a page from the Controller. So later on I'll pair this with a Controller to show you how to render the screen onto the View page we want.
We know the user requests a page from the Controller, so let's open
and tweak the code. If I want to throw out a specific page, I can use the
Normally, if you call it without any arguments, it'll automatically grab the template name matching the current action, for example it'll auto-fetch
. But that's kind of hard to grasp just by talking about it, and personally I prefer specifying the page myself, so let's actually write some code and try it out.
and paste in the example below:
language-php shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

<?php
namespace app\about\controller;

use think\Controller;

class HelloWorld extends Controller
{
    public function Hello()
    {
        return $this->fetch();
    }
}
Then open your terminal and run
. Next, open your browser and visit
Errors out saying it can't find the template
The reason is that we didn't specify a template, and the Controller function is named
, so ThinkPHP automatically goes looking for
. But we don't have that file yet, so let's create a
After revisiting, the View renders successfully:
But what if we want visiting
page instead? Then we just tweak
and pass the HTML filename we want to render into

<?php
namespace app\about\controller;

use think\Controller;

class HelloWorld extends Controller
{
    public function Hello()
    {
        return $this->fetch('HelloWorld');
    }
}
ThinkPHP comes with a built-in template engine, and it's really simple to use. You just wrap things in
inside your HTML. But make sure you actually pass the parameter, otherwise it'll error out. The way you send parameters to the front end is with

<?php
namespace app\about\controller;

use think\Controller;

class HelloWorld extends Controller
{
    public function Hello()
    {
        $name = 'iThome';
        return $this->assign('name',$name) -> fetch(); // assign('the parameter name you plan to send to the front end', value)
    }
}
// assign('the parameter name you plan to send to the front end', value)
and add this little line inside the body:
This is a parameter passed through the Controller: a Hello, {$name}!
If you don't want to use the
syntax for parsing, ThinkPHP actually gives you an option to change it. You'll find the relevant settings under
Personally I wouldn't recommend changing this option, since it'll just confuse whoever maintains the project later.

Going forward I'll gradually cover more HTML templating alongside the actual examples, showing which other HTML template features you can use. Basically anything you can think of, ThinkPHP's built-in HTML templating has it, like the usual loops and conditionals.
The official docs also offer a third-party template engine that works a bit like AngularJS's template engine. For the specifics, check out
There's also a Twig template engine option,
. Personally I lean toward sticking with native development, mostly because I'm not sure whether other template engines might have compatibility issues with ThinkPHP.
